title: TF-MAS: Training-free Mamba2 Architecture Search
abstract: The Mamba-type neural networks have gained significant popularity recently. To effectively and efficiently establish model architectures of Mamba, it is natural to introduce Neural Architecture Search (NAS) methods into Mamba. However, existing NAS methods tailored for Mamba are training-based, leading to substantial time and computational resource expenditure. To address this issue, and considering that Mamba2 is an improved version of the original Mamba, we propose a training-free NAS method specifically designed for Mamba2. Based on rank collapse in stacked State Space Duality (SSD) blocks, we design a proxy that only requires the computation of the transformation matrix and its gradient between two tensors within the network. Additionally, we develop a corresponding search space and introduce a novel approach for determining adjustable hyperparameter ranges. Experimental results show that our method outperforms all existing training-free NAS approaches in terms of both ranking correlation and the performance of search results for Mamba2 architecture. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first training-free NAS method designed for Mamba-type architectures. Our codes are available at https://github.com/fanyi-plus/tf-nas.

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
This paper, for the first time, propose a Zero-cost NAS for Mamba2 models. Using the rank-collapse property, the propose method desinged a new indicator which shows high rank correlation with the actual validation performance.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
Strengths
1. The paper is the first to propose a zero-cost NAS method specifically tailored for Mamba2 models.
2. The proposed indicator is thoughtfully designed, leveraging the unique structural properties of Mamba2 blocks.
3. New datasets are introduced to facilitate zero-cost NAS evaluation for Mamba2, which adds practical value.

Weaknesses
1. The reported rank correlation of the proposed method is approximately 0.68, which may be insufficient for reliable application in practice.
2. A correlation analysis focused on the top-K candidates would be more appropriate, as final model selection typically depends only on this subset.
3. In Eq. (6), the indicator aggregates values across blocks without normalization. A more general formulation would consider averaging to ensure comparability across different architectures.
4. Since Mamba2 is a variant of Mamba, the overall scope and practical applicability of the work may be somewhat constrained.

QUESTIONS:
Please refer to the weaknesses section and provide responses to each point.

LIMITATIONS:
The scope and practical applicability of the proposed method may be somewhat limited.
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
This paper introduces TF-MAS, the first training-free Neural Architecture Search (NAS) method specifically designed for Mamba2 architectures. The core contribution is a proxy metric based on rank collapse theory in stacked State Space Duality (SSD) blocks, which mirrors the rank collapse phenomenon observed in stacked attention layers. The authors develop transformation matrices W_X, W_B, and W_C that map from the input tensor U to the SSD inputs X, B, and C, handling three different cases based on the relationship between sequence length T and feature dimension W. The proxy combines nuclear norms of these transformation matrices with their gradients to estimate network performance without training.

The paper also presents a principled search space design with four adjustable hyperparameters (depth D, width W, state dimension N, and number of heads H), where ranges are determined by computational cost constraints rather than arbitrary choices. The authors construct NASBenches for Mamba2 models using weight entanglement and validate their approach through ranking correlation experiments and actual search results, demonstrating superior performance compared to existing training-free NAS methods adapted from CNN and Transformer domains.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
**Strengths**:

***Quality***: The theoretical foundation is solid, building on established rank collapse theory from attention mechanisms and properly extending it to SSD blocks. The mathematical treatment of the three cases (T=W, T<W, T>W) for computing transformation matrices is rigorous, with appropriate use of pseudoinverse for under-determined systems. The experimental validation spans multiple model sizes (130M to 2.7B parameters) and datasets, showing consistent improvements.

***Clarity***: The paper is generally well-written with clear motivation. The connection between attention rank collapse and SSD rank collapse is well-established, and the proxy computation process is explained systematically. Figure 1 effectively illustrates the method integration into Mamba2 architecture.

***Significance***: This addresses a genuine gap in the NAS literature - no previous training-free methods existed for Mamba architectures. The computational savings are substantial compared to training-based approaches, and the performance improvements over baseline Mamba2 models demonstrate practical value. The cost-driven search space design is a notable methodological contribution.

***Originality***: The work is highly original as the first training-free NAS for Mamba. The extension of rank collapse theory to SSD blocks is novel, and the systematic handling of different tensor dimension relationships shows theoretical depth.

**Weaknesses**:

***Quality concerns***: The paper contains critical mathematical errors that completely invalidate its theoretical foundation. The rank collapse proof in Equation (11) contains one typo (argmin should be min). More importantly, it contains a severe flaw: the best rank-1 approximation error can usually achieve a smaller composite norm than the original norm. This fundamental mathematical error undermines the core justification for why the proxy should work. Additionally, the reliance on weight entanglement for NASBench construction introduces potential bias, and ranking correlations are moderate (0.3-0.7), suggesting room for improvement.

***Clarity issues***: Some notation becomes heavy, particularly in the mathematical derivations. The relationship between the proxy computation and actual rank collapse could be made more intuitive. The search space construction process, while principled, involves several hyperparameters (0.6x and 1.6x scaling factors) that seem somewhat arbitrary.

QUESTIONS:
***Mathematical rigor and correctness***: The statement in Equation (11) of ||U||_{1,\infty} <= min_{x',y'} ||U-x'y'^T||_{1,\infty} does not have a proper justification and is very likely wrong (a simple counter example: U=[[2,0],[0,1]], ||U||_{1,\infty}=2, x'=(1,0)^T, y'=(2,0)^T, so ||U-x'y'^T||_{1,\infty}=||[[0,0],[0,1]]||_{1,\infty}=1). Can you provide a rigorous proof of this step or reformulate the approach? Additionally, your gradient calculations assume exact solutions (UW_X = X) but apply to approximate pseudoinverse cases in scenarios 2 and 3.

***Proxy design justification***: The proxy formula in Equation (6) combines nuclear norms of transformation matrices with their gradients, but lacks theoretical justification for why this specific combination should predict network performance. Why is the nuclear norm product the right choice rather than other matrix norms or combinations? How sensitive is the proxy to the approximation errors introduced in Cases 2 and 3 where exact solutions don't exist? Can you provide error bounds showing how pseudoinverse approximations affect proxy reliability?


***Weight entanglement validation***: The paper relies heavily on weight entanglement for NASBench construction but provides limited validation of its effectiveness for Mamba2. Can you provide more rigorous analysis comparing architectures trained from scratch versus those using weight entanglement? What is the correlation between their final performance, and how might this bias affect your conclusions?


***Computational overhead breakdown***: You mention 1.31 seconds for proxy computation, but how does this scale with model size and search space complexity? Can you provide a detailed breakdown of computational costs for transformation matrix computation, especially for the VWSSMamba2 search space with its exponentially larger size?

LIMITATIONS:
No - While the authors discuss some practical limitations and societal impacts, they miss a critical limitation that significantly affects the reliability of their work. The authors acknowledge experimental scope (language modeling only), reliance on weight entanglement, testing on smaller models, and provide thoughtful discussion of societal impacts including both benefits (medical diagnosis, autonomous driving) and risks (data privacy).

However, the authors fail to acknowledge that their core theoretical justification contains fundamental mathematical errors. The rank collapse proof in Equation (11) has critical flaws (type mismatches, incorrect inequality directions, logical gaps) that undermine the theoretical foundation of their approach. This represents a significant limitation in the validity and reliability of their method.
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
This paper proposes a training-free metric that ranks Mamba-like architectures that vary in terms of depth, width, state dimension and number of heads. The authors firstly identify the issue of rank collapse of the Mamba2 architecture, and based on this, propose a proxy metric that evaluates the degree of the collapse (hence trainability of the model). Afterwards, the authors evaluate the proposed proxy by measuring the rank correlation between the proxy scores and the accuracy of the sampled models on various reasoning datasets. To search for the best architecture in the design space, an evolutionary algorithm, that utilizes the proxy as fitness value, is used. The final benchmark results are promising and the authors conduct analysis and ablation studies on their algorithmic design choices as well.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
**Strengths:**

- The proposed approach proposes a new zero-cost proxy in order to automatically evaluate Mamba2 architectures with varying depth, width, state dimension and number of heads. The idea is novel and an interesting research direction.
- The problem tackled by the paper is significant and more research towards automating such design choices in subquadratic models is in general necessary in my opinion.
- The writing is clear and the paper is well-structured, including background and related work, theoretical justifications of their design choices and experiments including standard benchmark results and ablations.
- Empirical results on commonsense reasoning datasets (Table 2) show promising results.

**Weaknesses:**

- The proposed solution to mitigating the problem of rank collapse and the architectural search space are simple and not novel per se (refer to [1] and [2] as rightfully referenced by the authors). Moreover, is there any reason why skip connections are not included in the architectural design space? In [2], it was shown that skip connections play a crucial role in mitigating rank collapse in Transformers.
- Table 1 shows the rank correlation between the proxy values and the true performance of the sampled architecture. Despite the results being better than the other baselines, it still seems that the rank correlation coefficient is not high enough in some tasks in order to for the proxy to be a reliable metric.
- The experimental results do not include multiple runs. As far as I know, the Mamba training can be unstable sometimes and therefore I see the necessity of reporting multiple runs in the experiments. Before evaluating the proxy on expensive benchmarks, I would recommend the authors to evaluate on smaller synthetic ones, such as the MAD benchmark [3]
- The authors have not released the code, however they mention in the Checklist that they will do so upon acceptance. I think this is a very important part since the paper is also proposing new benchmarks in addition to the methodology.

QUESTIONS:
- In case 1 of Section 3.1, the authors assume that the input elements are normally distributed. How relevant is this assumption and is it violated in practice?
- Is the TF-MAS proxy specifically designed for Mamba2 architectures or is it possible to use that on linear attention Transformer search spaces as well? 
- Why did the authors pick the Mamba2 architecture when recent work on subquadratic models [4, 5] has shown better performance compared to Mamba2?


-- References --

[1] Dong et al. Attention is not all you need: Pure attention loses rank doubly exponentially with depth. In ICML 2021

[2] Zhou et al. Training-free Transformer Architecture Search. In CVPR 2022

[3] Poli et al. Mechanistic Design and Scaling of Hybrid Architectures. In ICML 2024

[4] Yang et al. Parallelizing Linear Transformers with the Delta Rule over Sequence Length. In NeurIPS 2024

[5] Yang et al. Gated Delta Networks: Improving Mamba2 with Delta Rule. In ICLR 2025

LIMITATIONS:
The authors do not discuss the limitations of their method in detail. Is the limitation of the method being applied only on Mamba2 architectures (mentioned in the Conclusion section) the only one?
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
This work introduces the first training-free NAS method tailored for Mamba2, an improved version of the Mamba neural architecture. By leveraging rank collapse in SSD blocks, the method uses a lightweight proxy based on transformation matrix gradients. A custom search space and hyperparameter strategy are also proposed. Experiments show superior performance over existing training-free NAS methods in both ranking accuracy and final architecture quality.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
Strengths:  
1. This paper has strong novelty and limited generality.
2. The paper proposes the first training-free NAS method for Mamba.
3. The search space design is interesting but quite complex, which may hinder adoption.

Minor Concerns:
1. Can you clarify how the method could be adapted when no suitable reference model exists for calculating the search space range?
2. Given the complexity of your pipeline, do you plan to release any tools or code to help practitioners adopt your method more easily?

Comment:
Given these strengths and limitations, I lean toward a borderline accept.

QUESTIONS:
as my concerns

LIMITATIONS:
yes
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
DECISION:
Accept (poster)

COMMENT:
A summary of the strengths and weaknesses based on the reviews and rebuttal (including the follow-up discussion among the reviewers and authors) is provided below.


**STRENGTHS**

(1) This paper proposes the first training-free metric for NAS in Mamba2 architectures (Reviewer kmdV,yy6s,vzNA), potentially inspiring future research along this line of work.

(2) The proposed problem is significant (Reviewers kmdV,yy6s).

(3) The paper is written well (Reviewers kmdV,yy6s).

(4) Empirical results are promising with new datasets introduced (Reviewers kmdV,vzNA).


**WEAKNESSES**

(1) The proposed solutions to mitigate the issue of rank collapse and the architectural search space are not new (Reviewer kmdV).

(2) The rank correlation coefficient is not sufficiently high in some tasks for the proposed proxy to be considered a fully reliable metric (Reviewers kmdV,vzNA).

(3) The proposed method has limited applicability as it can only be applied to Mamba2 architectures (Reviewers kmdV,vzNA).

(4) Initially, Reviewer yy6s has pointed out an error about equation (11), which has since been corrected by the authors in their rebuttal.


Weaknesses 1, 2, and 3 are acknowledged by the authors in their rebuttal to Reviewer kmdV.

From the above, the pros (especially strength 1) outweigh the cons.

The authors are encouraged to revise their paper based on the above feedback and that of the reviewers.

Minor note:
The authors can consider providing some more references to the conventional training-free NAS papers, such as the ones below (and see the references therein), and describe how the proposed approach fundamentally differs from such works (besides the difference in models):

Zero-cost proxies for lightweight NAS. ICLR 2021.

Robustifying and Boosting Training-Free Neural Architecture Search. ICLR 2024.

Neural architecture search on ImageNet in four GPU hours: A theoretically inspired perspective. ICLR 2021.

Unifying and Boosting Gradient-Based Training-Free Neural Architecture Search. 2022

Towards NNGP-guided Neural Architecture Search. arXiv 2020.

NASI: Label- and Data-agnostic Neural Architecture Search at Initialization. ICLR 2022.
reviews.TITLE: Decision
